title: The Solitary Self
text:
[Music]
[Music]
Um well, it's delightful to be invited
to come to the RSA and
I look forward to a good bit of
discussion. I'm told I don't have to
talk for 45 minutes but for a short time
and that suits me much better. Um this
is a book about
individualism. Um and it is written out
of intense uh indignation and
exasperation uh with the kind of
individualism which is being sold today
under the name of Darwin. It's not what
Darwin said. Um but that on its own uh
wouldn't be my business. I'm not a
Darwin scholar. Um but I think that the
state of individualism is a worry to all
of us. Um and it's good
to get it straight. Um you see I think
if one means by individualism the you
know the regard to separate individuals
instead of just to the mass. Um this has
of course been a splendid contribution
of the enlightenment. Uh something that
we probably all uh think terribly highly
of and very important. Um but like a lot
of things uh it's one individualism is
something you can have uh too much of um
if you go on in the direction in which
you have started to um change society
cutting everybody off more and more from
everybody else. uh forgetting about the
uh communal side of life uh you get into
a great mess in private life. Private
life tends to lose meaning. Um and
public life of course doesn't work so
well. This is a a cliche stuff. Uh but
somehow we seem to have been forgetting
it.
Um Darwinism is now used as the name for
this kind of thing. Um people think of
Darwin as having um insisted that
evolution only works by competition and
the survival of the fittest and dog eat
dog and so forth. Well, it so happens
that in the descent of man, Darwin, far
from this had a very interesting view
about how we are social creatures and
like other social creatures um naturally
cooperative and friendly. Even though of
course we can quarrel but quarreling is
part of being social. Um
the discussion that he gets into in the
descent of man it starts by asking
um really for the origins of morality.
How come uh we as all humans are subject
to this thought that what we've done
isn't what we ought to have done. He
says the mysterious word ought um
bothers people. Uh remorse is a very
widespread human thing. Um even the
mafia you see have their standards uh
standards are a kind of universal thing
and it is a very odd one um looked at
from as it were ethological point of
view as a bit of natural history. It's
pec peculiarity of the species and what
he says about it really is a bit
different from what a lot of other
people talking about morals have said um
because they tend to say well we have
all these animal instincts which are
pretty disorganized and often rather
bad. We also have our reason or our
conscience which is a quite separate
affair that comes in and straightens it
up. Darwin doesn't look at it like that.
He says we have a lot of natural as he
calls social instincts, emotional
tendencies and so forth. Some of which
are
um helpful to other people and some are
not. Um but they often conflict one with
another. This of course also happens to
other social animals. uh one chimpanzeee
may get very cross with another with
whom they're otherwise friendly and may
u beat them up. When that has happened,
they do in the end usually become
reconciled. But the way in which they
become reconciled is that the one who's
been beaten up comes and apologizes,
comes and says, "I'm so sorry." And then
the one who beat them up uh says, "Okay,
let's get together again." And everybody
gets together. And nobody, the other
chimpanzees, you see, are not saying,
"You shouldn't have done that." It
really is a distinctive feature
ethologically speaking of our species
that this kind of thing goes on. And
what Darwin suggests you see is that
there is not this separate character
called reason or conscience uh which
we've somehow developed and nobody else
has. um but that in becoming more
intelligent we become more aware of our
lives as a whole and in fact more you
know a veggger memory and a bigger
anticipation of our past and future
selves. So we are looking at the whole
thing in a way that other creatures
don't because of this intelligence and
therefore we're aware of the conflicts
which the other creatures had not
noticed and we are therefore put in the
position of having to do something about
it. Thus we invent our various kinds of
morality. This is the briefest possible
sketch of what Darwin says. Um and you
see I devoted quite a lot of the book to
saying what is wrong with um extreme
neodyarwinism of present day. And I have
chiefly talked about the selfish gene,
not because I've got a special grudge
against Dart Dawkins, but because he's
so eloquent and has been so popular, is
so popular um that he has got this
across uh to an immense public. And I
keep noticing how people who are not
supposed to be interested in science at
all when they're talking about how some
um some self-interested and
disastrous act say. Well, of course
that's that's human nature. It's
evolution, isn't it? You see, there's a
sort of fatalism generated uh by this
line of talk. Dawkins didn't invent it
any more than Maggie Thatcher invented
it.
The suggestion I'm making about it is
this that during the second world war
and for quite a time after it uh there
was quite a strong sociable
u conscientious feeling about society as
a whole. Uh people were not at all
ashamed to shout about the importance of
loyalty and the common good and so
forth. Um that persisted as I say for a
little time after the war. Thus, we got
the National Health Service and similar
things, but people were getting a bit
bored with it towards the end of the
60s. They thought they'd been rationed
for long enough. Uh they didn't want to
go in on eating the national loaf. Do
you know that very healthy national
loaf? I happen to remember it got rid of
I noticed at the time because people
weren't supporting it and so forth. Um
there was a
general a general movement of
consciousness towards thinking that we
should each of us should be considered a
little
more. I still think it's surprising that
the word selfish caught on with the
selfish gene to the extent that it did.
But I think that Dawkins in using that
word fully understood what the times
were demanding. He the fashion was
inside him. He didn't mean to make a
great social statement. He was trying to
talk about evolution. This was his first
book. It was the lectures he'd been
given to giving to his students and it
had plainly gone down a bomb. And it was
it seemed to him to be a
straightforward description of what
happens in evolution, but in fact it was
madly not. Um I mean the the the the
idea that the gene is selfish uh isn't
at all persuasive because genes work
along with other genes. Afterwards after
that book Dawkins noticed that and on
the 25th anniversary of the selfish gene
when there was great celebration going
on he said oh it's quite a good book. I
think it would have been better if I'd
called it the cooperative
gene. You see, because by then I mean
the fact that this is simply not science
had got through. Well, so if it wasn't
science um why was he saying it? Because
I'm thinking this is a myth which was
catching on with a lot of people. But he
put it so very forcibly. Uh this if
anyone feels that I am being um unfair
in picking on him, this is why I do
sometimes use other people to quote. But
you see, he's such a he's such an
impressive
writer. You kind of come back to it. Um
how am I doing for time? You've got
another 10 minutes. Uh right. Yes.
So
um yes I mean I'm thinking you see that
this this general reversion to a an
individual is a strong individualist
point of view um again wasn't new. There
is a strong tradition of this in our
culture dating I suppose from Thomas
Hobbes. Now, Hobbes did a smashing job
in the 17th century um reacting against
the wars of religion which were going on
at that time um saying there's no reason
why you should uh go and uh die or
suffer for other people in this kind of
war. Uh the what is most concerned to
you always is your own survival. And if
you can't um survive and do what people
are telling you, you have to do
something else. there
was I'm sure similarly um a reaction was
beginning and Hobbes kind of spotted it
and phrased it terribly forcibly and
well um and on the whole the point that
he made that um against the feudal
situation in which you naturally do uh
what your lord tells you and you move as
a kind of as part of a a great chunk of
people who all move together. the idea
that each person should think well am I
going to survive if I do this uh that's
my prime not interest um that was I
think a new a new thought in the 17th
century and of course it's been a
tremendously fertile one I mean all this
about individual rights that we've gone
in for since then um did kind of flow
from it again I'm not saying hobbs
invented it he just put it very well and
very strongly um and
that tradition went on. Um, after a time
people uh rather abandoned Hobbs's idea
that the best way to survive was to
support an absolute monarch because
absolute monarchs didn't always help
people to survive. Uh, and the the
business of the social contract which he
kind of launched uh was developed in the
thought that you you you you only have
to sacrifice yourself if you've
consented to it. Um well that's all
splendid stuff.
Um that tradition of course uh has gone
on but something a little different
happened in the area of self-respect and
so forth. Um after a time when uh
the civil wars of the 17th century were
over and life got a bit easier and more
established um people began to take it
for granted that they would survive.
They weren't so worried about that
because they weren't their lives weren't
being threatened all the time. They got
more interested in having a fulfilling
life, a life that they had chosen here
and now.
And you see a quite new element when got
into the egoistic tradition uh with
niche that is nature is extremely keen
on our all um choosing on our own
exactly how we wish to live. It's a
matter of being
authentic
of choosing your course rather than
having somebody else choose it for you.
Uh the business of independence
uh is worked out in much more
thoroughess. Now
um the effect is that as it were a
hamzian egoist may make very different
demands on society from a nichian
egoist. But a nichian egoist is perhaps
in a way uh not so likely to make
demands to society as to go off and live
on a mountain or um in some other
solitary place which of course was what
nature did himself.
Um so that I mean you see what I think
is quite interesting
the the egoist tradition has been
developed in many very sophisticated
ways since Hobbes and similar people
started it. It's not in the primitive
state that it was in in the 17th
century. What distressed and bored and
bothered me about the selfish gene was
it seemed to ignore all that. Uh it
didn't take account of the various
things that it might mean you see
for someone to be acting in their own
interest. Um it took it for granted that
it's all a matter of
of not even of surviving but of having a
suitable number of descendants.
um it defi defines selfishness in this
terribly narrow
and really unfertile sort of way. Um and
um it's not of course only Dawkins that
need it. As I say, there's a whole
mythology
um
of talk about evolution in terms of the
competitive in
investments which everybody's making.
It's all pretty commercial and grudges
and suckers and uh the like that the the
the metaphors are extremely lively. Now
in this morning's discussion on start
the week somebody said oh but aren't you
being unfair to Dawkins this is only a
metaphor metaphors are not only um
anyone who chooses one and runs it uh
strongly in their work is saying
something um even if they don't say it s
explicitly it the choice of a metaphor
is a most responsible choice um and you
see I'm saying that the choice of this
term the selfish gene is really
extraordinary seeing that genes are not
solitary things. Um and there's
notoriously a rather strange transition
from the genes being selfish to the
people being selfish. But it is also
said that the people are selfish. Um the
other way in which I'm constantly told
I'm unfair to Dawkins is people say oh
he's not a fatalist. He's not uh really
saying that this is inevitable because
he says we have free will. Now this is a
reference to his well-known policy of
open manly
self-contradiction. He's not the only
one who does this. BF Skinner was very
hot on it too. um if you're saying
something which is plainly contrary to
what everybody believes
and I think they live by you just
suddenly put in of course not that
that's not true that's not what I'm
saying and you don't argue you just go
straight back to what you were saying
before um which in the case of the
selfish gene is that we're uh blundering
robots or lumbering robots that's right
all you see all organisms are supposed
to be lumbering robots being run by
their genes. Um, and if having written
that you think, oh well actually this
isn't true because people have free
will, you've got to alter what you wrote
and explain
why. He never does. So I mean this is an
an objection which I reckon somebody
will be about to make. So I'm just
saying uh what I think about it in
advance. Um I'm having certain amount of
difficulty remembering all that I put in
the book in 20 minutes. Um it might not
be a bad idea I think uh to pause at
that point. Thank you very much. Thank
[Applause]
[Applause]
you. Um I'm going to ask uh Mary a
couple of questions and then open it up
to uh to to to all of you. And and we've
got until uh 7:00. Thank you for that,
Marian. Um, as you say, you only cover
um a small amount of the rich material
in the book, so I might pick up a couple
of other uh things. Could I start off
with asking you about accounts of human
nature? Because in a way, this book is
about human nature. Yeah. All my work is
about human nature. Well,
right. Do you think it's possible to
have a holistic account of human nature
which is
which works in relation to the kind of
criteria we'd apply to scientific
reasoning and to social scientific
reasoning and to philosophy. Is it
possible to bring those together in a
kind of unified account of human nature
or does science do what science does?
Social science does what social science
and that they're kind of in a in the end
they're kind of parallel types of
conversation. Ah this is a question
about the relation between the different
disciplines between the different ways
of thinking altogether isn't it? Uh yeah
um now my view about that is that these
different ways of thinking are very
important have to be the way to be
separate. Um they are the ways that we
use when we approach a subject in a
particular way for a particular kind of
reason. Thus, if you look
at say Newton's thought from the point
of view of thought and you're thinking
of other theories, you're talking about
the thought the theory uh if you're
interested in his life, you come in from
a different angle. Um these things are
not rivals. uh that they need to to
bring them together um is always a bit
of a special job in which both I think
both disciplines have to take part don't
they? Um yes I mean this is the sort of
trouble that comes up um with
physicalism that is uh people who say
that everything is really physics and
the phys the physical truth about
everything is is is the real one
everything else is rather superficial
and provisional in halfway. Now that's
not sense. Um
uh if you inquire uh for instance about
um about sport
u we want to know what sport is so to
speak. If you only knew about sport that
it is the movement of certain physical
particles uh within a certain space you
would not know anything about sport. Um
and see I mean the suggestion that if we
only had physics that would be we would
have all the reality uh the rest doesn't
matter is merely silly. Um but the the
the if you develop both of these
accounts you will get uh some idea of
the physical effect on the bodies which
is in indeed a bit of biology. Um and
you will also be looking at the
psychological and social meaning of
this. Now it's terribly important that
you get both isn't it? Um and uh this is
always setting us problems in bringing
them together. Um but
um I don't the thing I particularly
don't like is the approach to human
nature which says there must be one
central explanation of it. you see um
the kind of well I don't want to be
crude about Freud but the Freudian
approach it's pretty well all sex
essentially um or the the Marxist
approach that it's it it it's all the
desire for for wealth um these are
really not helpful um and there have
been too many uh sort of confident
attempts to explain human nature explain
it in the sense of finding one one one
pattern which is but is really essential
to it. I don't uh have much belief in
them. Great. Thank you.
Um in the book, one of the and also in
your talk, one of the arguments that you
want to confront is the argument that as
it were, there is a human nature which
isn't somehow kind of selfish or base
and then reason comes along as a kind of
higher faculty which helps us to deal
with the fact that we are uh selfish um
uh and uncontrollable.
Um I'm interested in you kind of
reflecting on that but also exploring
the idea that what is different about
human beings is that we can reflect on
our nature and indeed that we can change
our nature through reflection. Yeah. So
for example we can understand that there
are idiosyncrasies generated by the fact
that we have prehistoric brains trying
to cope with a modern world and
understanding those idiosyncrasies we
can uh override them as it were.
And that this faculty, this capacity to
reflect on our nature and to change our
nature is something which is very
distinctive about human beings. Well,
it's something which is very important
about human beings um which we kind of
need to cultivate. Um when people say
it's special about human beings, that
seems to suggest that no other animal
has it. And I don't feel that confident
about what life is like for elephants.
You know, u pick on elephants. They live
as long as we do. They mourn their dead
friends. They communicate in ways which
we do not understand. I simply don't
feel confident in saying that an old
elephant as he walked away having dealt
with the bones of his dead friend
mightn't say it's funny we do this. You
know, um become aware of I I don't think
it matters, you see. Um and I don't
think
um I mean people have been so
frightfully uh obsessed and bothered
about this thought that they have to
prove that we're different from all the
other animals um in all the ways that
are honorable about us. Now you see
actually what Dorid what Darwin is
saying what Darwin is saying in the
descent of man is uh really of great
interest here because he says you know
the social instincts that we have
including you see love and affection and
friendship and all these very honorable
things we do share with the other
creatures in social creatures in many
ways. um but we give ourselves this
extra difficulty and these extra
opportunities by being more aware of
them. So the awareness indeed is very
important but uh that there to say that
it's unique to people I really don't
feel confident. And then final question
for me before I before I open it up. Um
I'm sure it's an apocryphal tale, but
I'm you may have heard the the the story
of TS Elliott getting into a cab and the
cab driver turning around to him and
saying, "Aren't you TS Elliott, you
know, the world's leading living poet?"
And Elliot said, "Yes." And he says, "I
had that Bert and Russell in the back of
my cab last week." Um and I didn't think
much of him. And Elliot says, "Well, why
not?" And the taxi driver says, "Well,
because I turned to Bert Russell and I
said, "Aren't you Bert Russell the
world's leading philosopher?" And he
said, "Yes." And then I said to him,
"Well, what's it all about?"
Um and Russell was unable to answer. Now
uh the reason which is you know uh the
reason I asked the question is because
one of the one of the other really
intriguing parts of the book is when you
talk about the notion of
purpose and it seems to me that you are
steering a course between the kind of
neodyarwinian view or the scientific
view or the scientist view there is no
purpose. There is just what happens as
genes fight it out. there is no nothing
underlying
um life as it were versus the kind of um
classic religious view that everything
has a purpose and it is all related to
the kind of master the plan. I'm
interested in you kind of because you
didn't talk about it in your in your
talk this kind of notion of purpose and
how we might understand purpose in
nature. Yeah. Well,
um, the
neodyarwinian view, the Dawkins type
view is an extremely confident statement
that there is no meaning. Um, and that
everything is caused by accident. I
don't think the word accident makes much
sense here. An accident is something
which within an ordinary ordered causal
sequence um is an interruption comes in
from somewhere else. An accident is an
interruption in a series. To say that
everything is accident makes no sense as
far as I know. Um and what they mean of
course uh they're shooting at religion
all the time which I find boring. Um and
they what they mean is that there isn't
a person um who's done it. We use the
word accident in human life when we mean
that nobody did it. Well okay that's not
the question. Um the question is about
order about whether there appears to be
an order here. Now Darwin, who indeed
had uh dropped the Christianity that he
was brought up with uh raised the
question in his autobiography of
um belief in God and said um one um
argument for it of an intellectual kind
seems to me very strong. that is I
cannot think and it is he says not only
difficult but impossible to conceive the
immense order that we see without
thinking of it as in some way connected
with something like a human mind. That's
not saying there's a chap out there that
did it. It's saying it is a kind of
order which is also shown in the human
mind. Uh there is something akin in the
order of our mind to the order of the
universe. That seems to me to be rather
good sense.
